metadata.name: Alicia Gonzalez
metadata.protocol: 4-direction expert panel verification (Demographer, Behavioral Economist, Political Scientist, Psychologist)

direction_1_seed_vs_interview.description: Seed data compared against interview transcript for consistency, enrichment, and contradiction.

demographer.detail: Seed states age 29, female, divorced, Chico CA 95928, bachelor's in STEM, computer/information research scientist. Interview confirms all: DOB July 14, 1996 (Q85), references being 29 (Q05, Q43), divorced ~2 years ago (Q01, Q84), apartment off Mangrove Ave in Chico (Q08, Q51), BS in Computer Science from Chico State (Q90), research scientist at lab in Chico (Q16, Q65).

Seed states 'Colombian household, parents who immigrated to the United States before she was born.' Interview enriches: father Carlos (60) from Bogota, mother Rosa (57) from a town near Cali, family lived in Sacramento area, parents became citizens when Alicia was a child, brother Diego (25) in LA. Mom worked at daycare (Little Stars), dad is maintenance supervisor at Valley Properties for 20 years. Maternal grandmother (82) still in Colombia (Q01, Q05, Q06, Q97-Q107).

Seed mentions 'bilingual Colombian household' and 'cumbia and vallenato.' Interview elaborates: Spanish spoken exclusively at home per mother's insistence ('En esta casa se habla espanol'), code-switching between school and home, church attendance at St. John the Baptist (Spanish-language Mass with Colombian priest Father Ruben), extended family network in Sacramento area (tia Lucia, tia Marta, cousins Sofia and Andres), weekly FaceTime with abuela (Q01, Q05, Q06, Q47).

Seed states 'Chico, California' as location. Interview enriches with 7 years of context: grew up in Sacramento, attended Chico State, stayed after graduation, chose lower-paying research position over Bay Area industry job. Apartment off Mangrove between downtown and university. Born in Sacramento, CA (Q87). Lived in Sacramento at age 16 (Q97).

Seed states education as 'bachelors' in 'stem.' Interview specifies Bachelor's in Computer Science from Chico State (Q90). Father completed high school in Colombia (Q100), mother did some college in Colombia in early childhood education but didn't finish (Q101). Alicia considering PhD (Q91, Q108).

Seed states marital_status as 'divorced.' Interview provides extensive elaboration: married at 23 to college boyfriend, together ~3 years total, married less than 2, divorce finalized ~2 years ago (~age 27). Career choice was a contributing factor (Q01, Q02, Q84).

behavioral_economist.detail: Seed describes career choice broadly ('competitive drive' + 'relaxed, sometimes disorganized work style'). Interview provides a pivotal revealed-preference moment: Alicia turned down a software engineering job in San Jose (~40% more pay, stock options) for a lower-paying research position under Dr. Patel in Chico. This demonstrates strong intrinsic motivation and willingness to accept large income penalties for autonomy and intellectual freedom (Q02, Q02 follow-up).

Seed mentions no financial information. Interview provides full financial picture: gross ~$5,800/mo (~$69,600/yr), take-home ~$4,200/mo, rent $1,250, student loans $18,000 ($220/mo), emergency fund at ~2.5 months expenses, retirement contributions at 3% employer match, savings rate $300-$400/mo, no credit card debt. Financial management via spreadsheet, no-credit-card-debt rule, mothers envelope-system influence (Q56-Q64, Q72-Q83).

Seed mentions 'modest travel budget.' Interview quantifies: conference travel partially lab-funded (registration, sometimes partial travel), personal Colombia trip budget $2,000-$2,500, reimbursement lag of $800-$1,000 creates cash-flow pressure, cuts eating out and non-essentials before conferences (Q62, Q81).

Seed persona describes 'relaxed, sometimes disorganized work style' with 'project timelines occasionally slip.' Interview confirms: late-night work sessions, staying up until 1-2 AM, deadline-week schedule collapse (55-60 hr weeks), sleep deprivation cascades into diet deterioration (crackers/cheese, excess coffee). But also shows deliberate counter-measures: yoga, breathing exercises, learning to say no (Q15, Q32, Q50, Q68).

Seed describes startup ambition ('launching a startup that offers AI-driven creative tools'). Interview enriches: 20-page notes document, informal market research, recognizes need for co-founder with business skills, plans to build prototype within a year. PhD as potential enabling step. Concrete but pre-execution stage (Q108 follow-up).

political_scientist.detail: Seed contains no political information. Interview provides rich detail: votes in every election including local/special, leans Democratic but rejects strong party identity, issue-based voter (immigration, education, climate/wildfire, tech policy), reads CalMatters, NPR, voter guides, policy newsletters. Parents' naturalization story motivates civic participation (Q23-Q30, Q95-Q96).

Seed mentions 'navigating the innovative tech ecosystem of Chico' and 'champion inclusive research practices.' Interview reveals concrete civic actions: Technolatinas mentoring, tutoring center volunteering, lab reading group on AI bias and equity, blog post on algorithmic fairness, participation in peaceful local marches, writing to representatives about immigration bills (Q04, Q17, Q24, Q26-Q29).

Seed mentions 'online network of Colombian scientists' as community connection. Interview reveals this is a 200-member Discord server where policy discussions occur, especially around immigration and STEM visa issues. Combined with her engagement in Technolatinas and the lab reading group, her civic participation is substantially more extensive than seed suggests (Q07, Q10, Q24, Q48).

Seed mentions no racial or social justice perspectives. Interview reveals substantial engagement: experiences as a Latina in tech spaces (asked to 'prove' qualifications, only brown person at conferences), participated in BLM-related lab conversations, developed equity-focused research practices (bias audits, diverse training data), reads Latinx scholars Roberto Gonzales and Cristina Mora (Q26-Q30).

psychologist.detail: Seed persona describes 'fuses scientific precision with artistic chaos' and 'spontaneous bursts of creativity.' Interview provides behavioral texture: analytical-then-intuitive decision style (spreadsheet followed by gut check on a hike, Q03), performance anxiety manifesting as chest tightness and racing thoughts before deadlines/presentations (Q45), nighttime rumination about career progress (Q43 follow-up).

Seed mentions 'competitive drive.' Interview contextualizes: competitive primarily in board games ('something about board games flips a switch,' Q07 follow-up), peer comparison on career milestones causing distress (Q43 follow-up), but collaborative rather than competitive at work (Q77). Competition is self-directed and context-specific.

Seed persona mentions 'occasionally losing track of deadlines.' Interview reveals the post-divorce identity reconstruction: threw herself into work, rediscovered painting, joined hiking club, adopted Luna, reconnected with friends. Describes moving from 'unmoored' to 'grounded' over two years (Q01 follow-up, Q43). This is a significant resilience narrative not in the seed.

Seed mentions 'vivid imagination' and 'love for storytelling.' Interview reveals multi-modal emotional regulation: painting ('shuts off the anxious part of my brain'), cooking elaborate recipes as meditation ('can't think about anything else'), journaling for cognitive clarity, yoga for physical stress relief, verbal processing with trusted friends. Systematic coping toolkit developed largely post-divorce (Q38 follow-up, Q45 follow-up).

Seed does not mention vulnerability to loneliness. Interview reveals episodic loneliness since divorce, especially on unplanned weekend evenings. She has developed active counter-strategies: calling friends, going to coffee shops for ambient presence, reframing as 'chosen solitude,' relying on Luna. This is a significant behavioral pattern not in the seed (Q43 follow-up).

Seed does not address family caretaking orientation. Interview reveals anticipatory financial planning for parents' retirement, concern about father's knee problems and physical deterioration, monitoring mother's health avoidance behavior. Alicia carries intergenerational caretaking responsibility she frames as 'non-negotiable' (Q05 follow-up, Q35, Q36, Q40).

direction_2_interview_vs_extracted_profi.description: Interview transcript compared against extracted_profile for accuracy, omissions, and misrepresentations.

demographer.detail: Profile header demographics (age 29, female, divorced, Chico CA 95928, BS in Computer Science, computer/information research scientist) all match interview exactly (Q84-Q90, Q08, Q16, Q65).
Profile does not include date of birth (July 14, 1996) from Q85. Should be in demographics header.
Profile does not include race/ethnicity. Interview: 'I usually say Latina or Hispanic' (Q89), of Colombian origin (Q88). Should be recorded.
Profile does not include birth city. Interview states born in Sacramento, California (Q87). Should be in demographics.
Profile does not include Hispanic/Latinx confirmation. Q88 confirms Colombian origin. Should be recorded explicitly.

Profile does not include religion in header. Q94 and Q47 establish Catholic upbringing with continued occasional attendance at St. John the Baptist (Spanish-language Mass). Behavioral rather than labeled, but parish attendance should be noted.

behavioral_economist.detail: Profile accurately captures career-over-income revealed preference, intrinsic motivation, disciplined savings, spreadsheet tracking, no-credit-card-debt rule, and financial management approach. All match interview evidence from Q56-Q64, Q72-Q83.

Profile work_and_career section lists gross/take-home salary but does not capture the monthly spending breakdown: rent $1,250, groceries ~$350, car insurance/gas/utilities $50-$150 each, Luna expenses ~$60/mo, coffee ~$40/mo beans plus coffee shop visits, student loan $220/mo, health insurance $120/mo. Total normal spend $2,200-$2,500. This detailed budget picture is available from Q56-Q57.
Profile does not capture the retirement account with 3% employer match as a specific commitment device (Q61). It is mentioned in work_and_career but not framed as a behavioral strategy.

Profile does not capture the reimbursement lag problem: conference travel costs $800-$1,000 upfront before lab reimbursement, creating cash-flow pressure and triggering budget-cutting behavior (Q81). This is a significant financial friction.

Profile does not capture art print sales as supplementary income ($150 from prints, Q60, Q74). While minor and irregular, it represents a diversification behavior and connects economic activity to artistic practice.

political_scientist.detail: Profile accurately captures consistent voting, issue-based evaluation, lean-Democratic orientation, engagement with CalMatters/NPR/voter guides, and civic participation through Technolatinas and tutoring. Matches Q23-Q30, Q95-Q96.

Profile community_and_civic section does not capture Alicia's specific priority on algorithmic accountability and tech policy as a political concern (Q25). This is a distinctive policy interest emerging from her professional expertise.

Profile does not capture the Camp Fire (2018) as a catalytic event that shifted her political engagement toward local issues -- housing policy, wildfire preparedness, emergency management (Q25 follow-up). This is a defining political development.

Profile does not capture Alicia's cross-aisle engagement style: interacting with conservative ranchers and small business owners at hiking club and farmers market, finding common ground on local issues despite ideological disagreement, explicitly avoiding social media arguments (Q24 follow-up).

Profile does not capture her direct political actions: writing to representatives about specific immigration bills, helping tia Marta navigate vaccine misinformation in Spanish-language Facebook groups (Q24 follow-up, Q42 follow-up).

psychologist.detail: Profile psychologist summary accurately captures high openness, moderate-to-high conscientiousness undermined by night-owl tendencies, rich social network with solitude needs, family devotion, and multi-modal emotional regulation. Matches Q01-Q03, Q43-Q45, Q109.
Profile does not capture the 48-hour grace period after paper rejections as a structured coping mechanism (Q44 follow-up). This is a deliberate self-regulation strategy developed from experience.

Profile does not capture the specific vulnerability pattern: paper rejection + father's health flare-up + wedding anniversary converging in one week, leading to a 5-day withdrawal episode (Q44). The convergence pattern and recovery through friend intervention (Priya showing up with soup) is psychologically significant.

Profile does not capture Alicia's family-of-origin emotional processing norms: parents show care through actions rather than words (mom sends food, dad fixes things), neither would suggest therapy, 'handling things yourself' is the cultural norm. She is 'warming up to the idea' of therapy (Q45 follow-up). This cultural context shapes her help-seeking behavior.

Profile does not capture the self-worth pattern tied to career milestones: comparing herself to peers with professorships or startups, 'ambient hum of am I doing enough,' nighttime brain spirals about undone tasks (Q43 follow-up). This is a persistent source of distress.

Profile does not document alcohol pattern during divorce (nightly wine for several weeks) and her self-correction: recognized the pattern, pulled back, redirected to yoga and hiking (Q38). This demonstrates self-monitoring and deliberate behavioral adjustment.

direction_3_profile_internal_consistency.description: Extracted profile checked for internal contradictions, logical inconsistencies, and cross-section coherence.

demographer.detail: All demographic fields in header are internally consistent with the body of the profile. Age 29, Chico CA, divorced, BS in CS, research scientist -- referenced consistently across all 15 preference categories and 4 summaries.

Salary figure ($69,600/yr gross, $4,200/mo take-home) in work_and_career is consistent with the spending profile in home_and_lifestyle ($1,250 rent) and the savings capacity ($300-$400/mo) mentioned in behavioral_economist summary. The math holds: $4,200 - $1,250 rent - $220 loans - $120 insurance - ~$350 food - ~$300 utilities/gas/misc = ~$1,960, leaving room for $300-$400 savings plus incidentals.

Demographer summary states 'approximately $69,600 gross annually' derived from $5,800/mo x 12. Interview states take-home ~$4,200 and gross ~$5,800/mo (Q72). These are self-reported figures that are internally consistent but the gross annual could range from ~$69,600 to ~$70,000 depending on rounding. Not a material issue.

behavioral_economist.detail: Financial figures across sections are internally coherent: rent $1,250 + normal spending $2,200-$2,500/mo total against $4,200 take-home leaves $1,700-$2,000 for all other expenses and savings. Savings of $300-$400/mo plus $220/mo student loans plus retirement contributions all fit within this envelope.

Behavioral patterns are internally coherent: intrinsic motivation (choosing research over industry), disciplined savings (spreadsheet, no-debt rule), present-bias in sleep and self-care, identity-based motivation (scientist/artist/mentor). All form a unified behavioral portrait.

Profile behavioral_economist summary describes her as having 'limited financial margin' and emergency fund at ~2.5 months, but the work_and_career section implies some flexibility through the savings rate. These are not contradictory but the framing differs -- the economist emphasizes constraint while the career section emphasizes steady progress. Both are accurate.

political_scientist.detail: Profile's political characterization (consistent voter, issue-based, lean-Democratic, high civic engagement) is internally consistent across the community_and_civic preferences, political_scientist summary, and media_and_information_diet sections.

The community_and_civic section and political_scientist summary are mutually reinforcing: both describe active participation across multiple civic domains (voting, mentoring, tutoring, online communities, peaceful protests, workplace advocacy).

psychologist.detail: Psychological profile is internally coherent: high openness drives the art/science integration, novel-seeking, and diverse interests; moderate-to-high conscientiousness drives the savings, career commitment, and self-care routines; the tension between these (staying up late to create vs. needing to be at work by 8:30) is accurately captured as an ongoing struggle rather than a contradiction.

Health_and_wellness mentions sleep issues and neck tension; psychologist summary mentions performance anxiety and rumination; emotional_well-being data from Q43-Q45 connects all three. Cross-references are accurate and form a coherent stress-response pattern.

Psychologist summary rates Neuroticism as 'moderate' with 'effective coping strategies,' but the interview reveals several vulnerability patterns (loneliness episodes, 5-day withdrawal after convergent setbacks, nighttime rumination, peer comparison distress) that suggest the effective coping strategies are hard-won and not always sufficient. A more precise characterization would note that emotional regulation is effortful and context-dependent rather than stable.

The dual-identity theme (scientist/artist, Colombian/American) is consistently maintained across arts_and_culture, work_and_career, cultural_and_linguistic, and the psychologist summary. No contradictions in how these identities are presented.

direction_4_completeness.description: Profile checked against full 109-question transcript for missing information that should be captured.

demographer.detail: Date of birth (July 14, 1996) from Q85 not included in profile header.
Race/ethnicity (Latina/Hispanic, Colombian origin) from Q88-Q89 not included in profile header.
Birth city (Sacramento, California) from Q87 not included in profile header.
Hispanic/Latinx confirmation from Q88 not included in profile header.
Religion/spirituality (Catholic upbringing, occasional attendance at St. John the Baptist, Spanish-language Mass) from Q47, Q94 not in profile header. Only appears in preference sections.

behavioral_economist.detail: Monthly budget breakdown not aggregated: rent $1,250, groceries ~$350, student loan $220, health insurance $120, car insurance/gas ~$100-150, utilities ~$100, Luna ~$60, phone/internet ~$80, coffee beans ~$40. Total fixed ~$2,320-$2,370 against $4,200 take-home (Q56, Q57, Q63, Q40).

Seasonal spending variation not captured: summer electricity bills up to $180 vs. ~$60 winter (PG&E), Christmas gifts $300-$400, air filter costs during smoke season, conference travel reimbursement lag (Q59, Q81).
Art print sales as supplementary income ($150 from prints, Q60, Q74) not captured. Also sold prints to offset tire expense -- connects artistic activity to economic function.

Emergency fund target of 3 months expenses (currently at ~2.5 months, needs ~$1,500 more) from Q62 not captured as a concrete savings goal. Three parallel savings goals: (1) rebuild emergency fund, (2) Colombia trip $2,000, (3) long-term down payment $40K-$50K (Q83 follow-up).

Parents' financial situation and Alicia's anticipated role not captured: father's employer insurance has $3,000 deductible, mother delays checkups to save money, father not yet eligible for Medicare (needs to reach 65), Alicia and Diego discussed chipping in for retirement (Q36, Q40, Q05 follow-up).

political_scientist.detail: Camp Fire (2018) as political awakening event not captured in profile: transformed her political engagement from national to local, specifically around housing policy, wildfire preparedness, and emergency management (Q25 follow-up).

Specific political actions not fully captured: writing to representatives about immigration bills, helping tia Marta navigate Spanish-language vaccine misinformation on Facebook, voting for an independent city council candidate based on housing competence (Q24 follow-up, Q42 follow-up, Q95 follow-up).

Algorithmic accountability and tech policy as political interests not captured. She explicitly names this as a shift in her views: 'I've become more interested in tech policy, algorithmic accountability' (Q25). This connects her professional expertise to civic engagement.

Cross-partisan engagement style not documented: interacts with conservative ranchers and business owners at hiking club and farmers market, finds common ground on local issues, redirects from ideology to specific problems (Q24 follow-up, Q30 follow-up).

psychologist.detail: 48-hour grace period as structured rejection-coping mechanism not documented: allows 2 days of disappointment, then actively responds with revision plan and peer consultation. Developed after convergent setback episode (Q44 follow-up).

Convergent-setback vulnerability pattern not documented: paper rejection + father's health + anniversary week triggered 5-day withdrawal (binge TV, cereal for dinner, social isolation). Recovery catalyzed by Priya's unsolicited visit with soup (Q44).

Family emotional processing norms not documented: parents show love through actions not words (food, fixing things), neither would suggest therapy, 'handling things yourself' is cultural expectation. Alicia is gradually departing from this norm ('warming up to the idea' of therapy) (Q45, Q45 follow-up).

Career milestone comparison as persistent distress source not documented: sees peers with professorships or startups, feels 'pang,' nighttime spirals about incomplete tasks, 'ambient hum of am I doing enough.' Manages through yoga and journaling but not resolved (Q43 follow-up).

Alcohol self-monitoring during divorce not documented: noticed increased nightly wine consumption for several weeks, recognized the pattern, deliberately redirected to yoga and hiking. Demonstrates high self-awareness and proactive behavioral adjustment (Q38).

expert_summaries.demographer: Alicia Gonzalez is a 29-year-old (DOB July 14, 1996) divorced Latina woman of Colombian origin, born in Sacramento, California, to immigrant parents (father Carlos, 60, from Bogota; mother Rosa, 57, from near Cali). She holds a bachelor's degree in computer science from Chico State (STEM) and works as a computer and information research scientist at a small lab in Chico, CA (ZIP 95928), earning approximately $69,600 gross annually ($4,200/month take-home). She lives alone in a rented one-bedroom apartment off Mangrove Avenue ($1,250/month) with her cat Luna. She is Hispanic/Latinx of Colombian origin, fluently bilingual in Spanish and English, and maintains strong transnational ties through weekly FaceTime with her maternal grandmother (82, in Colombia), an online Colombian scientist community, and planned return travel (Q01, Q05, Q06, Q62, Q88). She grew up Catholic, attending Spanish-language Mass at St. John the Baptist parish in Chico occasionally, primarily for cultural and familial reasons (Q47, Q94). Her parents are working-class: father is a maintenance supervisor at Valley Properties in Sacramento (20 years, nearing retirement with physical health concerns), mother worked as a daycare teaching assistant and now does part-time babysitting/housekeeping. Her brother Diego (25) works in music production in LA (Q100-Q107). Her educational attainment represents upward intergenerational mobility -- her father completed high school in Colombia and her mother had some college -- and she is considering a PhD to further her trajectory. She resides in a mid-sized California college town chosen deliberately for quality of life, outdoor access, and research autonomy over higher-paying Bay Area opportunities (Q02), which constrains her professional network but enables an affordable lifestyle relative to coastal California.
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Alicia demonstrates strong intrinsic motivation over extrinsic rewards, as evidenced by the defining career choice to accept a ~40% pay cut for research autonomy and intellectual freedom over an industry software engineering position in San Jose (Q02). She employs disciplined financial management through a personal budget spreadsheet (updated weekly-biweekly), a strict no-credit-card-debt rule, and a retirement contribution matched at 3% by her employer (Q56, Q61, Q63). Her savings rate of $300-$400/month against $4,200 take-home reflects moderate-to-strong future orientation, with three parallel savings goals: rebuilding emergency fund to 3 months ($1,500 needed), Colombia trip ($2,000), and long-term home down payment ($40K-$50K) (Q62, Q83 follow-up). She manages cash-flow friction from conference reimbursement lags ($800-$1,000) through preemptive spending cuts (Q81). Seasonal cost variation is significant: summer PG&E bills spike to $180 (vs. $60 winter), Christmas gifts cost $300-$400, and air filters add recurring costs during wildfire season (Q59). She supplements income occasionally through art print sales (~$150, Q60). Present-bias manifests in sleep habits (staying up until 1-2 AM for creative work despite 7 AM wake-up) and deadline-driven self-care collapse (diet deterioration to crackers/cheese and 4-5 cups of coffee, Q32). She has developed counter-measures: Sunday meal prep, morning breathing exercises, learning to decline overcommitment (Q50). Her parents' financial precariousness -- father's $3,000 insurance deductible, mother delaying checkups, approaching retirement without adequate savings -- creates anticipatory caretaking spending that she and Diego frame as 'non-negotiable' (Q05 follow-up, Q35, Q40). Identity-based motivation drives career and financial choices: she builds her portfolio around being a scientist, artist, and community mentor, accepting income penalties that align with this self-concept rather than optimizing purely for financial returns.
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Alicia is a highly engaged civic participant who votes in every election including local specials and ballot measures, placing her in the most active segment of the electorate (Q23). She leans Democratic but rejects strong partisan identification, describing herself as 'left of center' with issue-based evaluation of candidates: immigration policy (personal family history), education funding and STEM access, climate/wildfire preparedness (Camp Fire 2018 as catalytic event), and algorithmic accountability emerging from her AI research (Q24, Q25, Q95-Q96). She exhibits high institutional literacy, reading CalMatters, NPR, Chico Enterprise-Record, the California voter guide cover-to-cover, and policy newsletters, while avoiding cable news for health information (Q23 follow-up, Q42). Her civic engagement extends well beyond voting: she mentors two undergraduates through Technolatinas (monthly one-on-ones, quarterly group meetups), volunteers as a math/programming tutor twice monthly, maintains a lab reading group on AI bias and equity, has written to representatives about specific immigration bills, helped her tia Marta navigate Spanish-language vaccine misinformation, and has participated in peaceful local marches (Q04, Q17, Q24 follow-up, Q29, Q42 follow-up). She practices deliberate cross-partisan engagement at the hiking club and farmers market, finding common ground with conservative rural community members on specific local issues while avoiding ideological debates -- 'we can disagree about a national movement but maybe agree that Chico's police review board should be more transparent' (Q24 follow-up, Q30 follow-up). The Camp Fire fundamentally redirected her political energy toward local governance: 'I realized that the stuff happening at city hall matters more to my daily life than most of what happens in Congress' (Q25 follow-up). Her trust in institutions is conditional and domain-specific: high trust in scientific and public health institutions (reads primary sources, CDC data, peer-reviewed journals), moderate trust in electoral processes, and nuanced skepticism toward law enforcement based on personal and family experiences (Q20, Q21, Q42).
expert_summaries.psychologist: Alicia exhibits high Openness to Experience expressed through intellectual curiosity across domains (ML/NLP research, generative art, DIY electronics, speculative fiction), aesthetic sensitivity (abstract painting in blues/oranges, attention to visual beauty in nature and algorithms), and active integration of dual cultural identities -- Colombian/American, scientist/artist (Q01, Q15, Q109). Her Conscientiousness is moderate-to-high but domain-specific: disciplined about financial tracking, savings, and career obligations, yet consistently undermined by night-owl creative sessions that sacrifice sleep (5-6.5 hours on bad nights, twice weekly) and occasional deadline slippage from exploratory detours (Q15, Q31, Q32, Q68). Extraversion is moderate: she maintains a rich social network (Priya, Tom, board game group, hiking club, Discord communities, family) and enjoys social engagement, but requires substantial solitude for creative and intellectual work and deliberately reframes post-divorce aloneness as 'chosen solitude' (Q07, Q11, Q43 follow-up). Agreeableness is high: she is mentoring-oriented, family-devoted, community-serving, and conflict-avoidant in cross-partisan interactions, though she speaks up on equity issues in professional settings when the context supports it (Q26 follow-up, Q28 follow-up, Q30 follow-up). Neuroticism is moderate and effortfully managed: performance anxiety before deadlines manifests as chest tightness and racing thoughts (Q45), nighttime rumination centers on career comparison with peers (Q43 follow-up), and convergent stressors can trigger multi-day withdrawal episodes (paper rejection + father's health + anniversary week led to 5-day isolation, Q44). Her emotional regulation toolkit is multi-modal and largely self-developed post-divorce: physical activity (yoga, hiking), creative expression (painting as anxiolytic, elaborate cooking as meditation), social support (verbal processing with Priya and Diego), cognitive strategies (journaling, gratitude practice, 48-hour grace period after rejections), and a morning breathing exercise (Q38 follow-up, Q44 follow-up, Q45 follow-up, Q50). Family-of-origin emotional norms emphasize action over verbal expression -- parents show love through food and physical help rather than emotional conversation, and therapy is outside their cultural frame -- but Alicia is gradually shifting toward openness to professional support, influenced by Priya's example (Q45 follow-up). She carries intergenerational caretaking responsibility (father's retirement planning, mother's health avoidance) that provides purpose but creates subtle burden (Q05 follow-up, Q35, Q40). Her motivational architecture is driven by curiosity ('the drive to understand things, to build things'), connection (family, community, mentees), and meaning-making (blending art and science, paying forward the mentorship she wished she had) rather than status or material accumulation (Q109). She demonstrates an analytical-then-intuitive decision style: gathers data systematically, then checks in with embodied feeling ('if something still feels wrong even when it looks right on paper, I listen to that,' Q03 follow-up). Post-divorce identity reconstruction has been largely successful, moving from 'unmoored' to 'grounded' over two years, though episodic loneliness and career-comparison distress persist as managed rather than resolved vulnerabilities (Q43).

inconsistencies_found.description: Psychologist summary rates Neuroticism as 'moderate' with 'effective coping strategies.' Interview evidence reveals coping is effortful and context-dependent: convergent stressors can overwhelm defenses (5-day withdrawal, Q44), nighttime rumination is ongoing (Q43 follow-up), and she has not yet sought professional support despite recognizing the potential benefit (Q45). More accurate to say 'moderate, with effortful and context-dependent regulation' rather than implying stable effectiveness.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Corrected psychologist summary to characterize emotional regulation as multi-modal but effortful, with acknowledged vulnerabilities to convergent stressors and unresolved career-comparison distress.

inconsistencies_found.description: Profile behavioral_economist summary describes 'limited financial margin' while work_and_career section shows steady savings progress. Not contradictory but framing differs. The margin is real ($400 emergency would 'sting' per Q82) but so is the discipline.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Corrected behavioral_economist summary to characterize financial position as 'disciplined with limited margin' -- acknowledging both the constraint and the active management.

inconsistencies_found.description: Profile states gross salary as ~$5,800/mo ($69,600/yr). Self-reported, internally consistent, but worth noting this is a rough estimate. Not a material issue.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: No correction needed. Retained as approximate figure with 'approximately' qualifier.

resolutions.action: Updated psychologist summary to state 'moderate, effortfully managed' Neuroticism with explicit acknowledgment of convergent-stressor vulnerability and unresolved career-comparison pattern.
Updated behavioral_economist summary to use 'disciplined with limited margin' framing.

verification.overall_verdict: The existing extracted_profile is substantially accurate and well-structured. No major contradictions were found between the seed data, interview transcript, and extracted profile. The profile correctly captures Alicia's dual scientist-artist identity, Colombian heritage, post-divorce lifestyle reconstruction, and core personality dynamics. The primary issues are: (1) several demographic fields missing from the header (DOB, race/ethnicity, birth city, Hispanic/Latinx, religion), (2) detailed monthly budget and seasonal spending variations not aggregated, (3) significant behavioral and psychological patterns from the interview not captured (48-hour rejection coping, convergent-stressor vulnerability, family emotional norms, career-comparison distress, Camp Fire political catalysis, alcohol self-monitoring), (4) political engagement depth understated (specific actions, cross-partisan style, tech policy interest), and (5) a minor neuroticism characterization adjustment from 'effective coping' to 'effortful, context-dependent coping.' All 109 questions were reviewed. The corrected profile addresses all identified gaps and inconsistencies while preserving everything that was accurate in the original.

corrected_extracted_profile.name: Alicia Gonzalez

health_and_wellness.summary: Active lifestyle centered on yoga, hiking, biking, and soccer, with ongoing sleep challenges, seasonal allergies, and deliberate stress-management practices developed largely post-divorce. Uses multi-modal coping (physical, creative, social, cognitive) with effortful regulation that can be overwhelmed by convergent stressors.
details: Attends vinyasa yoga classes twice weekly (Monday/Wednesday at 6:30 PM) at Chico Yoga Studio with instructor Mei; started ~2 years ago during marriage breakdown (Q15 follow-up)
Hikes most Saturdays through the Chico Outdoor Club in Butte County trails and Ishi Wilderness (Q07 follow-up, Q15)
Bikes as primary in-town transportation on a beat-up road bike; daily use for commute, errands, downtown (Q08 follow-up)
Returning to amateur soccer league this summer (played in college); Sunday morning pickup games to prepare (Q18)

Chronic undersleeping: typically 6.5 hours on good nights (midnight to 7 AM), 5 hours on bad nights (1:30-2 AM to 7 AM), bad nights ~twice weekly. Driven by night-owl creative sessions. Headaches and focus loss when sleep-deprived (Q31, Q31 follow-up)
Takes OTC antihistamine during spring allergy season (oak pollen in Central Valley); causes mild drowsiness; avoids morning hikes during peak pollen (Q31 follow-up)
Uses air purifier at home year-round for both allergies and wildfire smoke; keeps N95 masks stocked for smoke season (Q09 follow-up, Q31)
Morning 5-minute breathing exercise before checking phone -- recent addition (Q50)
Hourly stretch reminders set on computer per physical therapist recommendation; uses ergonomically adjusted chair/monitor and specialized neck-support pillow (Q33 follow-up)
Uses herbal teas (chamomile for sleep, ginger for digestion) and turmeric capsules for inflammation; combines traditional Colombian remedies with Western medicine (Q37, Q37 follow-up)
Mother Rosa's home remedies: agua de panela with lime for colds, chamomile tea and warm compress for stomachache, arnica cream for bruises (Q37)

History of severe neck/shoulder tension from prolonged computer work (~1 year ago); resolved with 6 sessions of physical therapy; ongoing management through stretching, yoga, ergonomic adjustments (Q33)
Stomach bug last winter that knocked her out for ~1 week (Q33)

Moderate social drinker: 1-2 craft beers or wine weekly, more on social weekends. During divorce, noticed increased nightly wine pattern for several weeks; self-corrected by redirecting to yoga and hiking (Q38)
Recreational marijuana use: handful of times, last ~4 months ago at friend's birthday (Q39)
No regular prescriptions; occasional ibuprofen for headaches (Q39)
Fully up to date on vaccinations including all COVID rounds; trusts CDC, peer-reviewed journals, and her doctor (Q41, Q42)

Stress eating pattern during deadline weeks: reverts to crackers/cheese at desk, frequent taqueria burritos, and dark chocolate from desk drawer; coffee intake increases from 2 to 4-5 cups (Q32 follow-up)
Performance anxiety before deadlines/presentations: chest tightness, racing thoughts, lying awake running scenarios (Q45)

Episodic loneliness since divorce, especially unplanned weekend evenings; manages through calling friends, coffee shop ambient presence, reframing as chosen solitude, Luna's companionship (Q43 follow-up)

food_and_dining.summary: Intermediate-to-advanced home cook specializing in Colombian dishes and fusion experiments, committed to fresh farmers market ingredients and craft coffee. Food serves as cultural connection, creative outlet, stress management, and social ritual.
details: Cooks traditional Colombian dishes: arepas, bandeja paisa, empanadas, chimichurri-marinated plantains (Q01, seed culinary_persona)
Grinds fresh Colombian single-origin coffee beans every morning from a local roaster; serious about coffee ritual (Q15)
Shops at the Saturday farmers market regularly; also uses co-op grocery store near apartment (Q08, Q15)
Frequents a taqueria near her apartment at least once a week; also a taqueria near the lab for lunch (Q08, Q15)
Meal-preps on Sundays when routine is stable; plans meals for the week, buys from farmers market (Q18)
Photographs dishes she is proud of; posts to Instagram (Q48)
Elaborate cooking as stress relief/meditation: 'Making empanadas from scratch... is so engaging that I can't think about anything else' (Q45 follow-up)
Stress eating pattern: reverts to crackers/cheese, taqueria burritos 3 days running, dark chocolate during deadline weeks (Q32 follow-up)
Coffee consumption: 2 cups normally, 4-5 during stressful periods (Q32 follow-up)
Monthly grocery spending: ~$350 (higher during farmers market stocking-up months) (Q56)
Coffee bean spending: ~$40/month plus occasional coffee shop visits (Naked Lounge) (Q57)
Mother Rosa sends food via cooler bag on the bus when Alicia can't visit (Q05 follow-up)

travel.summary: Budget-conscious traveler drawn to culturally immersive and rugged outdoor experiences, with strong desire to revisit Colombia and conference travel as regular professional obligation.

Saving $2,000-$2,500 for a return trip to visit grandmother (82) -- urgency driven by grandmother's advancing age (Q62)

Budget constraint: dreams of buying a house but down payment ($40K-$50K) is years away; travel spending carefully managed against savings goals (Q83 follow-up)

social_and_relationships.summary: Rich social life centered on close friendships, deep family bonds, and community involvement despite living alone post-divorce. Processes stress through verbal engagement with trusted friends and deliberate social outreach.
details: Closest friend Priya (data engineer, Indian American, met at hackathon 3 years ago): board game nights every other Friday, mutual support through 'immigrant family expectations in tech' bond, Priya was critical during emotional low point (showed up with soup, Q44) (Q07, Q44)

Near-daily phone calls with mother Rosa (57); sometimes real conversations, sometimes just daily check-ins (Q05)
Father Carlos (60): quieter, shows love through action (drives to Chico to fix things unprompted); more frequent calls since divorce (Q05, Q45 follow-up)
Visits parents in Sacramento ~twice monthly; holiday gatherings at parents' house (25+ people at Christmas) (Q05 follow-up)
Extended family in Sacramento: tia Lucia, tia Marta, cousins Sofia and Andres (around her age, group chat); dad's family mostly in Colombia (Q05 follow-up)
Abuela (maternal grandmother, 82, Colombia): weekly Sunday FaceTime calls; 'Mi nieta la cientifica' (Q06)

Friendly with immediate neighbors: the Hendersons (older couple, watch her place), Jenny (grad student below, shares leftovers) (Q08 follow-up)

Normal total $2,200-$2,500; spikes with tires ($400), conferences, or holiday gifts ($300-$400) (Q56, Q57, Q59)

arts_and_culture.summary: Active participant in Chico's art scene and deeply grounded in Colombian cultural traditions. Art practice integrates generative algorithms with physical painting, bridging her scientist and artist identities.

Algorithmic accountability and tech policy as emerging political interest from AI research work (Q25)

Member of Chico Outdoor Club (joined post-divorce via coffee shop flyer) (Q07 follow-up)

Wrote blog post on algorithmic fairness for lab website (Q27 follow-up)
Written to representatives about specific immigration bills (Q24 follow-up)
Helped tia Marta navigate Spanish-language vaccine misinformation on Facebook; walked through clinical trial data in person (Q42 follow-up)

Cross-partisan engagement: interacts with conservative ranchers/business owners at hiking club and farmers market; redirects from ideology to local specifics ('we can disagree about a national movement but agree that Chico's police review board should be more transparent') (Q24 follow-up, Q30 follow-up)

Practical, bike-friendly lifestyle suggests casual dress; no other fashion details emerged in 109 questions

summaries.demographer: Alicia Gonzalez is a 29-year-old (DOB July 14, 1996) divorced Latina woman of Colombian origin, born in Sacramento, California, to immigrant parents (father Carlos, 60, from Bogota; mother Rosa, 57, from near Cali). She is Hispanic/Latinx, fluently bilingual in Spanish and English, and grew up Catholic, now attending Spanish-language Mass at St. John the Baptist parish in Chico occasionally for cultural/familial reasons. She holds a bachelor's degree in computer science from Chico State and works as a computer and information research scientist at a small lab in Chico, CA (ZIP 95928), earning approximately $69,600 gross annually ($4,200/month take-home). She lives alone in a rented one-bedroom apartment off Mangrove Avenue ($1,250/month) with her cat Luna (Q01, Q05, Q08, Q47, Q56, Q72, Q85, Q87, Q88, Q90). Her parents are working-class: father is a maintenance supervisor at Valley Properties in Sacramento (20 years, nearing retirement at 62 with chronic knee problems); mother worked as a daycare teaching assistant at Little Stars for 12 years and now does part-time babysitting/housekeeping. Her brother Diego (25) works in music production in LA (Q100-Q107). Her educational attainment represents significant upward intergenerational mobility -- her father completed high school in Colombia and her mother had partial college. She maintains strong transnational ties through weekly FaceTime with her maternal grandmother (82, in Colombia), a Colombian scientist Discord community, and planned return travel. She resides in a mid-sized California college town chosen deliberately over higher-paying Bay Area opportunities for quality of life, outdoor access, and research autonomy (Q02), which constrains her professional network and dating pool but enables an affordable lifestyle relative to coastal California.
summaries.behavioral_economist: Alicia demonstrates strong intrinsic motivation over extrinsic rewards, as evidenced by the defining career choice to accept a ~40% pay cut for research autonomy over an industry software engineering position in San Jose (Q02). She employs disciplined financial management through a personal budget spreadsheet (weekly-biweekly updates, developed post-divorce), a strict no-credit-card-debt rule, and retirement contributions matched at 3% by her employer (Q56, Q61, Q63). Her savings rate of $300-$400/month against $4,200 take-home reflects moderate future orientation, with three parallel savings goals: (1) rebuild emergency fund to 3 months (~$1,500 needed), (2) Colombia trip (~$2,000), and (3) long-term home down payment ($40K-$50K) (Q62, Q83 follow-up). She manages cash-flow friction from conference reimbursement lags of $800-$1,000 through preemptive spending cuts (Q81). Her financial position is disciplined with limited margin: a $400 emergency is 'uncomfortable but not catastrophic' (Q82), and she carries $18,000 in student loan debt at $220/month (Q63). Seasonal cost variation is significant: summer PG&E bills spike to $180 from ~$60 winter, Christmas gifts cost $300-$400, air filters add recurring costs during wildfire season, and conference travel creates temporary deficits (Q59). She supplements income occasionally through art print sales (~$150 recently, Q60). Present-bias manifests in sleep habits (staying up until 1-2 AM for creative work despite 7 AM wake-up) and deadline-driven self-care collapse (diet deterioration, coffee escalation from 2 to 4-5 cups, Q32). Counter-measures include Sunday meal prep, morning breathing exercises, and learning to decline overcommitment (Q50). Her parents' financial precariousness -- father's $3,000 insurance deductible, mother delaying checkups, retirement planning uncertainty -- creates anticipated caretaking expenditures that she and Diego frame as 'non-negotiable' (Q05 follow-up, Q35, Q40). Identity-based motivation drives career and financial choices: she builds her life around being a scientist, artist, and community mentor, accepting income penalties that align with this self-concept (Q02, Q108, Q109).
summaries.political_scientist: Alicia is a highly engaged civic participant who votes in every election including local specials and ballot measures, placing her in the most active segment of the electorate. Her parents' naturalization story is a foundational motivation: 'My mom drove me to the polling place when I turned 18' (Q23). She leans Democratic but rejects strong partisan identification, describing herself as 'left of center' with issue-based evaluation of candidates across four core domains: immigration policy (personal family history, follows NILC and FWD.us, has written to representatives), education funding and STEM access, climate/wildfire preparedness (catalyzed by Camp Fire 2018), and algorithmic accountability emerging from her AI research (Q24, Q25, Q95-Q96). She exhibits high institutional literacy, reading CalMatters, NPR, Chico Enterprise-Record, the California voter guide cover-to-cover, policy newsletters, and researcher accounts on Twitter/X, while explicitly avoiding cable news for health information (Q23 follow-up, Q42). The Camp Fire fundamentally redirected her political energy toward local governance: housing, zoning, wildfire preparedness, emergency management -- 'the stuff happening at city hall matters more to my daily life than most of what happens in Congress' (Q25 follow-up). Her civic engagement extends well beyond voting: she mentors two undergraduates through Technolatinas, volunteers as a math/programming tutor twice monthly, maintains a lab reading group on AI bias, wrote a blog post on algorithmic fairness, has written to representatives about immigration bills, helped her tia Marta navigate Spanish-language vaccine misinformation, and has participated in peaceful local marches (Q04, Q17, Q24 follow-up, Q27 follow-up, Q29, Q42 follow-up). She practices deliberate cross-partisan engagement at the hiking club and farmers market, finding common ground with conservative community members on local issues while avoiding ideological debates and social media arguments entirely (Q24 follow-up, Q30 follow-up). Her trust in institutions is domain-specific: high trust in scientific and public health institutions (reads primary sources), moderate trust in electoral processes, and nuanced skepticism toward law enforcement based on personal experience (protest with heavy police presence) and family stories (father's traffic stops, cousin Andres's car searched) (Q20, Q21, Q42).
summaries.psychologist: Alicia exhibits high Openness to Experience expressed through intellectual curiosity across domains (ML/NLP research, generative art, DIY electronics, speculative fiction), aesthetic sensitivity (abstract painting in blues/oranges, attention to visual beauty in nature and algorithms), and active integration of dual cultural identities -- Colombian/American, scientist/artist (Q01, Q15, Q109). Her Conscientiousness is moderate-to-high but domain-specific: disciplined about financial tracking, savings, and career obligations, yet consistently undermined by night-owl creative sessions that sacrifice sleep (5-6.5 hours on bad nights, ~twice weekly) and occasional deadline slippage from exploratory tangents (Q15, Q31, Q32, Q68). Extraversion is moderate: she maintains a rich social network (Priya, Tom, board game group, hiking club, Discord communities, family) and thrives on connection, but requires substantial solitude for creative and intellectual work, deliberately reframing post-divorce aloneness as 'chosen solitude' rather than isolation (Q07, Q11, Q43 follow-up). Agreeableness is high: mentoring-oriented, family-devoted, community-serving, conflict-avoidant in cross-partisan interactions, though she speaks up on equity issues in professional settings (Q04, Q26 follow-up, Q28 follow-up). Neuroticism is moderate and effortfully managed rather than stably regulated: performance anxiety before deadlines manifests as chest tightness and racing thoughts (Q45), nighttime rumination centers on career comparison with peers who have professorships or startups (Q43 follow-up), and convergent stressors can overwhelm coping -- paper rejection + father's health flare-up + wedding anniversary week triggered a 5-day withdrawal episode with social isolation, binge TV, and cereal-for-dinner regression (Q44). Her emotional regulation toolkit is multi-modal and largely self-developed post-divorce: physical activity (yoga as 'breaking the cycle,' hiking as decompression), creative expression (painting as primary anxiolytic, elaborate cooking as active meditation), social support (verbal processing with Priya and Diego), cognitive strategies (journaling, 48-hour grace period after rejections, gratitude practice, morning breathing exercise), and self-monitoring (caught her own increased wine consumption during divorce and redirected to yoga/hiking) (Q38, Q38 follow-up, Q44 follow-up, Q45 follow-up, Q50). Family-of-origin emotional norms shape her help-seeking: parents show love through actions (food, fixing things) rather than emotional conversation, neither would suggest therapy, and 'handling things yourself' is the cultural expectation -- but Alicia is gradually shifting, influenced by Priya's positive therapy experience, and is 'warming up to the idea' (Q45, Q45 follow-up). She carries intergenerational caretaking responsibility (father's retirement, mother's health avoidance) that provides purpose but creates subtle burden (Q05 follow-up, Q35, Q40). Her motivational architecture is driven by curiosity, connection, and meaning-making rather than status or material accumulation -- 'the drive to understand things, to build things, to ask what if' (Q109). She demonstrates an analytical-then-intuitive decision style: gathers data systematically then checks in with embodied feeling ('if something still feels wrong even when it looks right on paper, I listen to that,' Q03 follow-up). Post-divorce identity reconstruction has been largely successful over two years, moving from 'unmoored' to 'grounded' (Q43), though episodic loneliness and career-comparison distress persist as managed rather than resolved vulnerabilities.

cultural_and_linguistic.description: Languages, cultural practices, religious/spiritual observance patterns, heritage markers
items: Fluently bilingual in Spanish and English; grew up speaking Spanish at home exclusively per mother's insistence ('En esta casa se habla espanol'); code-switched between home (Spanish) and school (English) without thinking about it (Q01 follow-up)

Colombian cultural identity expressed through cooking (bandeja paisa, arepas, empanadas), music (cumbia, vallenato), and family rituals (Sunday lunches, holiday gatherings with 25+ extended family, church followed by big lunch) (Q01, Q05 follow-up)

Attends Spanish-language Mass at St. John the Baptist parish in Chico occasionally (~once a month and holidays), primarily for cultural/familial reasons; congregation is mostly Latino families; priest Father Ruben is Colombian (Q47, Q47 follow-up)
Lights votive candles in apartment as personal spiritual ritual when loved ones face difficulties (Q47 follow-up)
Wears grandmother's crucifix necklace as heritage marker; 'more about connection to her than theological belief' (Q47)
Weekly Sunday FaceTime calls with maternal grandmother (82) in Colombia after church; 'Mi nieta la cientifica' (Q06)
Maintains Colombian identity through online Colombian scientist Discord community (~200 members), local cultural events, and volunteer tutoring programs (Q07, Q04 follow-up, seed cultural_background)
Last visited Colombia 3 years ago (Bogota, Cartagena, mother's hometown); planning return to see grandmother before she 'gets much older' (Q62 follow-up)

Family follows Colombian traditions: Carnaval de Barranquilla referenced in seed; Saturday morning cumbia and arepas; mother sends food via cooler bag on bus to Chico (Q01, Q05 follow-up, seed cultural_background)
Gratitude practice before hiking or painting: 'not prayer exactly, but intentional' appreciation of where she is (Q47 follow-up)
Finds spiritual awe in nature rather than traditional religious practice; 'standing at the top of a trail looking out over the valley' (Q47 follow-up)
Ethics from religious upbringing inform her commitment to fairness in AI, mentoring, and community service, even without traditional religious expression (Q47 follow-up)

life_events_and_transitions.description: Significant past or anticipated events that shape current behavior
items: Divorce finalized approximately 2 years ago after less than 2 years of marriage (married at 23 to college boyfriend). Catalyzed major identity reconstruction: threw herself into work, rediscovered painting, joined hiking club, adopted Luna, reconnected with friends. Describes moving from 'unmoored' to 'grounded' (Q01, Q01 follow-up, Q43)

Career crossroads post-graduation: chose lower-paying research position under Dr. Patel over ~40% higher-paying software engineering job in San Jose. Defining life decision, first crack in marriage, validated by mother ('Mija, you can always make more money'), father came around after seeing publications (Q02, Q02 follow-up)

Camp Fire (2018) profoundly affected regional community: displaced people from Paradise, housing crisis in Chico, emotional weight of smoke season; shifted her political engagement toward local governance (Q25 follow-up)
Considering PhD program as next career transition; would deepen technical expertise, expand network, potentially enable startup. Undecided on timing and financial implications (Q91, Q108 follow-up)

Father Carlos (60) approaching retirement with chronic knee problems and physical deterioration from 20+ years of maintenance work; hoping to retire at 62 but finances uncertain. Alicia and Diego plan to contribute; 'non-negotiable' (Q05 follow-up, Q35)
Grandmother's advancing age (82) creating urgency around planned Colombia visit; last trip was 3 years ago (Q62 follow-up)

Paper rejection ~6 months ago with harsh feedback, coinciding with father's knee flare-up and wedding anniversary week; triggered 5-day withdrawal. Recovery through Priya's intervention led to 48-hour grace period coping strategy (Q44, Q44 follow-up)
Neck/shoulder injury ~1 year ago from 3 consecutive days at desk without moving; resolved with 6 PT sessions; prompted ergonomic changes and yoga commitment (Q33)

sensory_and_physical_context.description: Non-clinical physical factors: chronic patterns, sleep, sensory environment, dietary practices tied to non-preference reasons
items: Chronic sleep insufficiency (~twice weekly): driven by night-owl creative sessions (painting, coding to 1:30-2 AM) against 7 AM wake-up. Has tried wind-down alarms but just snoozes them. Headaches and reduced focus when sleep-deprived (Q31, Q31 follow-up)

History of neck/shoulder tension from prolonged computer work; resolved with PT but requires ongoing management through hourly stretches, ergonomic chair/monitor, specialized neck-support pillow (Q33, Q33 follow-up)

Spring seasonal allergies (oak pollen in Central Valley): itchy eyes, runny nose, general fogginess. Daily OTC antihistamine causes mild drowsiness. Avoids morning hikes during peak pollen; keeps windows closed, runs air purifier (Q31 follow-up)

Wildfire smoke exposure (summer/fall): AQI can exceed 200, confining her indoors. Air purifier and N95 masks stocked. Smoke triggers emotional/collective response post-Camp Fire ('You see that haze and you think, is it happening again'). Plans indoor projects or coastal trips during peak (Q09 follow-up)
Chico heat: regular summer temperatures over 100-110 degrees; apartment has poor insulation, AC drives electricity bills to $180+ in peak months (Q10, Q59)
Sedentary job (8+ hours at computer) partially counteracted by yoga, hiking, biking. Tried standing desk but couldn't focus as well (Q32)

media_and_information_diet.description: News sources, social media platforms, entertainment formats, information-seeking behavior
items: NPR for national news and health coverage (Q23 follow-up, Q42)
CalMatters for California state policy (Q23 follow-up)
Chico Enterprise-Record for local news (Q23 follow-up)
Policy newsletters and researcher accounts on Twitter/X for tech policy and immigration; follows NILC and FWD.us (Q24, Q24 follow-up)
CDC publications and peer-reviewed journals for health/vaccine information; has training to read primary sources directly (Q42)
California voter guide read cover-to-cover before elections (Q23 follow-up)
Avoids cable news entirely for health information: 'Too much spin, too little substance' (Q42)
Curates social media feeds aggressively; mutes inflammatory political content; unfollows accounts that 'make me feel bad about myself or angry without being informative' (Q48 follow-up)
Spanish-language misinformation in Facebook groups identified as community challenge; personally intervened with tia Marta on vaccine hesitancy by translating clinical trial data (Q42 follow-up)
Podcasts from epidemiologists and public health researchers (Q42)
Reads Latinx scholars on race/identity: Roberto Gonzales, Cristina Mora (Q28)
Self-help/productivity content: reads practical articles and books from which she extracts applicable techniques (breathing exercise) (Q50)

technology_and_tool_use.description: Device preferences, app usage, digital communication norms
items: Instagram as primary art-sharing platform (~800 followers): paintings, generative art, food photos, hiking stories, time-lapse painting videos. Has connected with generative artists and received online group show invitations (Q48, Q48 follow-up)
Twitter/X for professional networking, science communication, and following researchers/journals. Shares papers, comments on AI ethics. Tries to avoid arguments (Q48)

Discord as primary community platform: Colombian scientists server (~200 members, close with ~12) and generative art channels. Vents about career frustrations, shares papers, gets emotional support after rejections (Q07, Q48, Q49)
Facebook used minimally: Chico Outdoor Club group and Colombian scientist group only (Q48)
Slack across 5+ workspaces: lab, UC Davis collaboration, professional communities (Q48)
FaceTime for weekly Sunday calls with grandmother in Colombia (Q06)
Zoom for remote research collaboration with UC Davis team (Q10 follow-up)
TurboTax for annual tax filing (Q75)
Personal budget spreadsheet (developed post-divorce, updated weekly-biweekly); tracks spending by category (Q56 follow-up)
App timers on social media: 20 min per platform per session (Q48 follow-up)
10 PM digital cutoff (70% compliance); intent is to have last hour be painting, reading, or quiet (Q48 follow-up)
Large-format printing for generative art pieces and mounted digital prints (Q17 follow-up)
Attempted alarm-based bedtime wind-down reminders; admits to snoozing them: 'It's a discipline issue, honestly' (Q31 follow-up)
